---
title: "Gender Inequality Data Analysis"
subtitle: "Executive Summary"
author: "Jinwook Bae, Miriam Haf, Xuchen Shen, Andreas Sünder"
date: today
date-format: "MMMM D, YYYY"
lang: en
fontsize: 10pt
linestretch: 0.95
format:
  pdf:
    papersize: a4
    geometry:
      - margin=1.5cm
header-includes:
  - \pagenumbering{gobble}
  - \RedeclareSectionCommand[beforeskip=.6\baselineskip,afterskip=.3\baselineskip]{section}
---

Gender inequality remains a prevalent issue in modern society, raising the question of how strongly this inequality is reflected in available data. This executive summary presents an overview of our team's data-driven analysis of biological gender inequality in education, care work, labor force participation, and the wage gap, and interprets their evolution over time across several countries.

We also analyzed correlations between related metrics to identify results that supported or contradicted our overall conclusions. Based on descriptive statistics and the available data, our team reached a reasonable overall assessment of the topic.

## Problem and Methodology

Data was often missing for different countries in different years across all relevant data sets, creating substantial sparsity issues. To ensure meaningful conclusions, our analysis used several approaches for cross-sectional data.

Where possible, we selected the single most recent year with sufficient data coverage, as shown in the GII analysis on slide 4 and the labor force participation rates on slide 16. When no single year provided sufficient coverage across countries, we defined a time window and extracted each country's most recent available data point within that range. This maximized the number of represented countries, as in the care-related labor inactivity analysis on slide 13.

When data sparsity was most severe, we first computed the median across all available data points for each country and then aggregated these country medians by region to derive a regional median. The resulting regional estimates are visualized by gender on slide 11 and shown as a gap on slide 21. To assess the reliability of country median data points relative to the regional median, interquartile range error bars (Q1 to Q3) are displayed. These indicate the dispersion of country-level medians within each region and reflect the number of countries contributing to each estimate.

Although this two-step approach did not always lead to concrete conclusions, it still indicated general inequality patterns that were then examined further for countries with sufficient data points. In cross-sectional analyses and in correlation plots between metrics, we only included countries with more than two data points over time. For time series analyses, we selected countries from different regions that offered sufficient data coverage over time.

## Outcome and Outlook

The GII values on slide 4 partially contradict the field-specific analyses. In education, females and males tend to participate in and complete different education levels at almost equal rates in developed countries, with slight female dominance at higher education levels.

The other fields more closely follow the general GII pattern. Females spend more time on care work in all regions, and a larger share of females is outside the labor force due to care responsibilities compared with males. In labor force participation, the same inequality appears across all selected countries and across education levels, with males generally showing stronger labor market participation.

In income, most regions show wage inequality disadvantaging females. However, when looking at specific countries, some countries with lower GDP and higher GII appear to show relatively better female pay outcomes. This suggests that economic growth alone does not necessarily lead to more equal wage outcomes. This is reflected in the weak correlation on slide 23, where the GII, as a general inequality indicator, shows little relationship with the wage gap itself.

For future work, a deeper analysis of inequality size and economic shifts may help explain why inequality indices fluctuate across regions. A GII-GDP correlation analysis could further clarify how economic status relates to gender inequality. In addition, the GII includes a health component that we did not analyze in this project and that remains open for further interpretation.
